query: common parasitic cysts in stool microscopy

retrieved_docs.title: Overview on Ordering and Evaluation of Laboratory Tests -- Specimen Requirements and Procedure -- Fecal Specimen
retrieved_docs.content: Physical examination of feces comments on color, odor, consistency, parasites, and pH. Microscopically, we can see food residues, muscle fibers, fats, fatty acid crystals, starch particles, cellulose residues, cells (RBCs, leukocytes, and epithelial), crystals (triple phosphate, calcium oxalate, cholesterol), ova and cysts of parasites, motile amoeba (containing ingested RBC, pseudopodia), mucus, and foreign bodies (hair, wool, etc.).

retrieved_docs.title: Entamoeba coli Infection -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Ova and parasite testing can be performed to help distinguish between different amoebae species. Testing via microscopy will show the presence of protozoa; however, it is essential to differentiate between Entamoeba coli and the pathogenic amoeba, Entamoeba histolytica . [23] Stool examination via ova and parasite testing is the most common form of analysis and evaluation. [4] [23] Stool examination with ova and parasite testing typically requires three stool specimens for best sensitivity and specificity. [2] Wet smears may result in difficulty in observing the Entamoeba coli cyst due to the refractile wall. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Entamoeba histolytica Infection -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Colonoscopy with histologic examination: Not used for routine diagnosis but can be used to look for other causes of symptoms.  Used to look for characteristic findings of amebic ulcers or erosions with the identification of trophozoites or cysts with biopsy specimens. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatic Cyst (Archived) -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: In general, non-infectious hepatics cysts are most prevalent in the female population. Their incidence also tends to increase with age. On the contrary, ciliated hepatic foregut cysts occur more commonly in men. Echinococcal hepatics cysts (also known as hydatid cysts) are more common in their endemic geographical regions which include South America, the Middle East, and the eastern Mediterranean, some sub-Saharan African countries, western China and the former Soviet Union. [2] Biliary cysts can present in infants due to congenital abnormalities. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Helminthiasis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Stool microscopy is sufficient in uncomplicated cases of trichuriasis. Colonoscopy can detect trichuriasis in severe cases, and sometimes biopsy also be needed to confirm the diagnosis. In trichuriasis dysentery syndrome, iron-deficiency anemia should be evaluated. [3] [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Fascioliasis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Microscopic examination of stool or duodenal bile aspirates for eggs is still useful for detecting chronic infections in rural or third-world settings where resources can be scarce. They are also sometimes found incidentally while working up biliary obstructions. Fasciola hepatica eggs are yellow-brown and ovoid measuring 130 to 150 micrometers long by 60 to 90 micrometers wide. A single adult fluke can release over 20,000 eggs per day, but that release is intermittent. Thus, an examination of multiple stool or duodenal aspirate specimens may be needed as one negative examination does not necessarily exclude the diagnosis. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Ascariasis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A female worm can produce up to 200,000 eggs a day. This makes retrieval from a stool exam easier. The best diagnostic test is still the stool exam for ova and parasites, searching for large oval brown trilayered eggs with a mamillated coat. It is important to note that stool can be negative while the worm migrates and matures (approximately one month). Only when worms are mature do they start secreting eggs. Sometimes an adult worm can be seen in the stool or coming out of the rectum but can also be coughed up or passed in the urine. A complete blood count can show eosinophilia during the active migration phase from the intestine to the lungs, and larvae can be found in the sputum. Abdominal x-rays can be sensitive but not specific when a whirlpool sign is present. Ultrasound and CT scans can be used to identify worms in the biliary duct and gallbladder. Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) can be used for diagnosis and treatment. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Giardia Lamblia Enteritis (Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Giardia Lamblia enteritis is diagnosed definitively through microscopic identification of the pathogen in a stool sample. Because Giardia is often shed intermittently, examining stool samples collected over a period of several days is most sensitive. [7] The traditional ova and parasite exam includes a permanent stained smear; however, because the microscopic examination takes time, resources, multiple samples, and a trained eye, immunoassays have become the main diagnostic tool.

retrieved_docs.title: Giardiasis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Human infection occurs through ingesting cysts via contaminated water or direct person-to-person contact, with transmission heavily influenced by inadequate hygiene and sanitation practices. Daycares have emerged as epicenters of infection due to insufficient handwashing protocols, especially during diaper handling and changing. [14] Within the intestinal system, cysts undergo excystation, giving rise to trophozoites. These trophozoites are identifiable as pear-shaped flagellated protozoa with 2 nuclei.

retrieved_docs.title: Sarcocystis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Fecal studies can include different flotation techniques, modified Kato thick smear, formalin-ether technique, or direct smear. [1] [19] Both oocysts and sporocysts can be detected in fecal samples from definitive hosts using stains such as periodic acid-Schiff stain. [18] The mature sarcocysts in each species tend to vary in size, length, and circumference with varying thicknesses of the sarcocyst wall, but all contain numerous bradyzoites. Muscle biopsy light microscopy can show the sarcocyst in muscular sarcocystosis, which may or may not show bradyzoites. [1] Imaging, including magnetic resonance imaging, may help detect muscle cysts and direct biopsies. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Echinococcus Granulosus -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Imaging is extremely useful in detecting and monitoring hydatidosis cases, especially those that are seronegative. Ultrasonography is the preferred modality for liver and intraabdominal hydatidosis, but its accuracy remains operator-dependent. It remains the modality of choice for screening due to its accessibility and portability. It's also helpful in post-treatment monitoring of the disease. For hepatic echinococcosis, the World Health Organization Informal Working Group (WHO-IWGE) developed an ultrasound classification to stage the disease [10] : CE 1: unilocular fluid collection/simple cyst with double line sign. CE 2: multivesicular/multiseptated cyst. "honeycomb" or "rosette-like." CE 3A: a fluid collection with a detached membrane (water lily sign). CE 3B: the presence of daughter cysts in a solid matrix. CE 4: cysts with a heterogeneous hypoechoic/hyperechoic matrix without daughter cysts. CE 5: solid cystic wall.

retrieved_docs.title: Strongyloidiasis (Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Sporadic excretion of larva in stool makes microscopic stool testing a very unpredictable and low-yield diagnostic tool. It is somewhat mitigated by serial stool testing. The sensitivity of serial stool samples reaches 100% when 7 samples, each from different days, are tested. [15] Several techniques have been applied over the years to enhance the detection of larvae in stool, including the Baermann method and the Harada- Mori method. [16] The blood agar plate culture method has the highest sensitivity ( 96%) among stool exam techniques. [17] Serologic testing by recombinant antigens is more sensitive but suffers from a lack of availability, particularly in endemic areas in the developing world. The rSs1a recombinant antigen has demonstrated a sensitivity of 96% and specificity of 93%. [18] Microscopic visualization of the larva can also be used for other body fluids like bronchoalveolar lavage fluid, cerebrospinal fluid, pleural fluid, or even duodenal biopsy. [19] Real-time polymerase chain reaction can also be used for body fluid samples and stool.

retrieved_docs.title: Giardiasis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Traditionally, giardiasis diagnosis involved identifying Giardia trophozoites or cysts through stool studies. However, more objective techniques, such as nucleic acid amplification techniques (NAATs), are now widely used. [28] Stool antigen enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays are also available. [29] These tests benefit screening in high-incidence situations, such as during an epidemic or in daycare centers. However, they should not replace stool microscopy. Stool antigen detection assays and NAATs are typically quicker, more sensitive, and more specific than microscopy. [30] Detecting Giardia through microscopy can be challenging as the protozoa only intermittently shed. The sensitivity of microscopy can be increased by collecting 3 stool samples on different days. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Amebiasis(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: If the parasite reaches the bloodstream, it can spread through the body and end up in the liver, causing amoebic liver abscesses. Liver abscesses can occur without previous diarrhea. Diagnosis is typically by stool examination using a microscope. An increased WBC count may be present. The most accurate test is specific antibodies in the blood.

retrieved_docs.title: Amebiasis(Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: E. histolytica is a pseudopod-forming, protozoal parasite that causes proteolysis and tissue lysis. Humans are the natural hosts. Amoebic infection occurs by the ingestion of mature cysts in fecally-contaminated food or water or from the hands. Excystation of the mature cysts occurs in the small intestine, and trophozoites are released; the trophozoites then move to the large intestine. The trophozoites increase by binary fission and produce cysts. Both stages pass in the feces. The cysts can survive days to weeks in the external environment because of the protection provided by the cyst wall. The cyst is responsible for further transmission of the parasite. Ingestion of only a small number of organisms can cause disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Duodenal Biopsy -- Clinical Significance -- Giardiasis
retrieved_docs.content: Giardiasis is a global parasitic scourge especially prevalent in parts of Russia and presents in those drinking unprocessed water such as mountaineers and travelers. Infected subjects may be asymptomatic or complain of intermittent bloating and watery diarrhea. It is usually diagnosed by stool studies, but occasionally the trophozoites are noted on the duodenal surface. Savvy pathologists in endemic areas can readily diagnose this with appropriate staining. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2648
retrieved_docs.content: The set of allowed qualifiers in the context of parasitology encompasses several distinct clinical entities: a bladder mass (قيلة مثانية), a cystic kidney condition (بيلة سِّيسْتينِية), mitral valve stenosis (تضيُّق الصمام المترالي), and a rectal mass (قيلة مستقيمية). In addition, the descriptor “Without” is specifically associated with both the bladder mass and the rectal mass, indicating that these two lesions can be present in the absence of certain accompanying features. Together, these relationships highlight that within parasitological considerations, the aforementioned four conditions are recognized as permissible qualifiers, while the presence of a bladder or rectal mass may also be noted when other expected findings are lacking.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2540
retrieved_docs.content: planning, diagnostic work‑up, and parasitological evaluation to a wide spectrum of congenital, metabolic, infectious, endocrine, renal, ophthalmic, reproductive, and neurological conditions, thereby supporting comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1458
retrieved_docs.content: informs preventive strategies, and guides targeted therapeutic interventions. The association of abdominal pain with malignant hypertension exemplifies how severe hypertension may manifest with gastrointestinal discomfort, highlighting the need to consider overlapping pathophysiological and parasitic mechanisms when evaluating the aforementioned conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2890
retrieved_docs.content: parasitic factors may contribute to or modify these diseases. Moreover, parasitology is specifically linked to nervous system congenital anomalies, indicating a possible etiologic role of parasites in congenital neuro‑developmental disorders. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that the allowed qualifier unifies diagnostic imaging, surgical planning, diagnostic evaluation, and parasitological investigation across a wide spectrum of endocrine, metabolic, renal, ophthalmic, obstetric, cardiovascular, neurological, and infectious conditions, guiding comprehensive patient assessment and interdisciplinary care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 4 -- Community 2773
retrieved_docs.content: Parasitology is recognized as an allowed qualifier for both the perineum and lip tumor, indicating that parasitic considerations are relevant when describing conditions affecting these anatomical sites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1590
retrieved_docs.content: Other infections, including parasitic infections, are classified as a subcategory under the broader parent disease group داء الريكتسيات.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2573
retrieved_docs.content: Anal and rectal conditions are encompassed within the broader category of gastrointestinal disorder (اضْطِراب مَعِدِيّ مِعَوِيّ). As a subset of this wider gastrointestinal spectrum, anal and rectal conditions affect the distal portion of the digestive tract and share many pathophysiological mechanisms, diagnostic considerations, and therapeutic strategies with other gastrointestinal disorders. Recognizing that anal and rectal conditions have a broader relationship with gastrointestinal disorder encourages clinicians to adopt a comprehensive, system‑based approach to evaluation and management, ensuring that patient care addresses both localized anorectal pathology and its context within the entire gastrointestinal system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2291
retrieved_docs.content: Cystic fibrosis without meconium ileus is recognized as a presentation that maps directly to the broader diagnosis of cystic fibrosis, indicating that the absence of meconium ileus does not preclude the condition and should be considered within the spectrum of cystic fibrosis manifestations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 210
retrieved_docs.content: modalities, procedural interventions, and disease processes are anatomically and clinically related, providing a comprehensive framework for the evaluation and management of abdominal, hepatic, and biliary disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1982
retrieved_docs.content: Constipation (إِمْساك) is clinically associated with vomiting (تَقَيُّء), indicating that patients who experience difficulty passing stool may also present with episodes of emesis, reflecting a possible shared gastrointestinal dysfunction or underlying condition that links these two symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1850
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal pregnancy (حمل منتبذ) is clinically associated with laparotomy (شق البطن), because surgical entry into the abdominal cavity is frequently required for the diagnosis, evaluation, and definitive management of this rare ectopic gestation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2169
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhoids encompass presentations that are described as بواسير غير محددة من دون ذكر مضاعفات, which translates to unspecified hemorrhoids without mention of complications. This indicates that cases of hemorrhoids that lack detailed specification or documented complications are still classified within the broader condition of hemorrhoids, guiding clinicians to consider appropriate evaluation and management even when the presentation is nonspecific.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1354
retrieved_docs.content: Broxyquinoline is classified as a member of the hydroxyquinoline derivatives used for the treatment of amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1689
retrieved_docs.content: Arsenic compounds, for amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases, are classified as agents against amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2608
retrieved_docs.content: Mesenteric pregnancy is classified as a disease of the digestive system, indicating that this rare form of ectopic pregnancy occurs within the mesentery and involves pathology of the gastrointestinal tract.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1851
retrieved_docs.content: The peritoneum serves as the anatomical finding site for فتق البطن (abdominal hernia) and is also the location implicated in اضطراب صِفَاقِيّ (serosal disorder), indicating that peritoneal involvement is central to both the presentation of abdominal hernias and the development of serosal disturbances.
